turns.zh: 好的，所以我要深入探讨一下，我只想简单提一下，我永远念不对他的姓，也许你能念对，但与其念错，Chamath——大多数人知道我说的是 Chamath。是的，Chamath。他说过，世界上第一个万亿富翁将是解决气候变化或对气候变化做出巨大贡献的人。所以，我认为，在目睹了过去几年的巨变之后，确实存在一些巨大的机会，对于——我们就称之为——想要高投资回报的冷血资本家来说。当然。现在有很多技术和解决方案正在开发中，如果谈到直接面向消费者或消费者使用的产品，消费者会选择它们而不是替代品。所以我认为这个领域变得非常令人兴奋。你提到了——现在有些东西看起来很明显，并且被广泛讨论。比如，太阳能的采用，也许政府补贴以减少对化石燃料和外国能源的依赖，特别是外国能源。但是，我从未意识到，正如你描述的，从 A 点到 Z 点，由于效率低下损失了如此多的能量。有哪些不拥挤或被低估的目标，或者说与气候变化相关的领域，你认为人们应该更多关注？

turns.en: There is what you can do personally and there's what it can be done professionally. And if we look at, you know, the obviously there's EVs and doing the right thing there and doing the your recycling and composting and all the right stuff at home and not, you know, reuse, don't buy all new. So, there's all those kinds of consumer-facing things that need to happen. But, it's really incumbent on the innovators, the companies, the individuals in the world to bring new solutions to consumers or change out the system without the customers even knowing.
turns.zh: 有个人能做的，也有专业能做的。如果我们看，显然有电动汽车，做正确的事情，在家里进行回收和堆肥，以及所有正确的事情，比如重复使用，不买全新的。所以，所有这些面向消费者的事情都需要发生。但是，真正有责任的是创新者、公司、世界上的个人，为消费者带来新的解决方案，或者在客户甚至不知道的情况下改变系统。

turns.en: To make things more efficient because customers don't know that 60% of all the energy created in the world is lost. What can they do about it? It just shows up. So we have to go and reboot our infrastructure in some ways, in many ways, in almost every way. And so there's one for me that we're now finally getting into, which is the hydrogen economy. If we think about all these different chemicals that we use today, they come out of petroleum. They come from sucking something out of the ground and then refining it, etc. Plastics is one of them. We can move to a hydrogen-based economy to create all of those products. We can create them more quickly with no or little CO2, get similar products out, and they're actually more profitable for the companies themselves. So there are all these things around hydrogen. Let's take fertilizer. It doesn't sound sexy, but we need a lot of fertilizer in this world. There are other ways of tackling that too, but fertilizer is a huge one. Concrete is another one. Concrete is huge. Steel, another one, right? Thermal, just creating thermal, like high-temperature steam and those kinds of things for different industrial processes. Today we burn coal, natural gas, or whatever. We could do it with hydrogen or we could do it with electricity. That whole area is unloved. People are starting to work on it. They're starting to make inroads there, but we need a lot more work on those things. It's not just electric cars. It's not just the things that you hear about in the press every day. So I think we need to be really thinking about the hydrogen economy and to a certain extent the biomass economy because we can actually turn a lot of the things we use today, like jet fuel, from bio sources, trash sources, not edible sources, but really trash or waste, and turn those into things that can stay carbon neutral. It's not a perfect solution because we're using things on the ground on the planet, biomass, but it's just sitting there and it's going to turn into CO2 or methane, so we could put it back in and use those things now. And we have lots of technologies to do that. So I think hydrogen economy, biomass economy, not chopping down trees and just lighting up smoke. That's not what I'm talking about here. I'm saying transforming it into chemicals and raw materials so that they can be used, not just burning wood, which we see a lot of people fudging that they say that's clean energy. It's not.
turns.zh: 为了提高效率，因为客户不知道全球产生的能源中有 60% 被浪费了。他们能做什么呢？能源就这么出现了。所以我们必须在某些方面、在很多方面、几乎在所有方面重新启动我们的基础设施。对我来说，有一个领域我们终于开始涉足了，那就是氢经济。如果我们想想今天使用的所有不同化学品，它们都来自石油。它们是从地下抽出来的东西经过精炼等过程得到的。塑料就是其中之一。我们可以转向基于氢的经济来生产所有这些产品。我们可以更快地生产它们，几乎不产生或很少产生二氧化碳，得到类似的产品，而且对公司来说实际上更有利可图。所以围绕氢有很多事情。以化肥为例。听起来不性感，但这个世界需要大量化肥。还有其他方法可以解决这个问题，但化肥是一个巨大的领域。混凝土是另一个。混凝土规模很大。钢铁也是，对吧？还有热能，就是产生高温蒸汽之类的东西用于不同的工业过程。今天我们烧煤、天然气或其他东西。我们可以用氢或电来实现。整个领域都不受重视。人们开始着手研究，开始取得进展，但我们还需要做更多工作。这不仅仅是电动汽车，也不仅仅是你在媒体上每天听到的事情。所以我认为我们需要认真考虑氢经济，在某种程度上还有生物质经济，因为我们可以把今天使用的很多东西，比如喷气燃料，从生物来源、垃圾来源（不是可食用来源，而是真正的垃圾或废物）转化过来，变成可以保持碳中和的东西。这不是一个完美的解决方案，因为我们使用的是地球上的生物质，但它就在那里，会变成二氧化碳或甲烷，所以我们可以把它重新利用起来。我们有很多技术可以做到这一点。所以我认为氢经济、生物质经济，不是砍树然后点燃冒烟。那不是我在说的。我说的是将其转化为化学品和原材料以便使用，而不是仅仅烧木头，我们看到很多人糊弄说那是清洁能源。其实不是。

turns.en: Let's return to a term that you used earlier that I would love to hear you speak about just a little bit, carbon removal. I've become very interested in the last 6 to 12 months in different means of carbon removal. Not carbon credits, not offsets, but actual carbon removal. I've looked very closely at what Stripe has done, which I think is really innovative and inspiring. I would love to know if you have any general thoughts about carbon removal, if any particular approaches or companies you find particularly interesting, any comments or thoughts at all on carbon removal?
turns.zh: 让我们回到你之前用过的一个术语，我很想听你谈谈，那就是碳移除。在过去 6 到 12 个月里，我对不同的碳移除方法非常感兴趣。不是碳信用，不是抵消，而是真正的碳移除。我仔细研究了 Stripe 的做法，我认为这非常创新且鼓舞人心。我很想知道你对碳移除有什么总体看法，是否有任何特别有趣的方法或公司，或者任何关于碳移除的评论或想法？

turns.en: Okay. So there's carbon removal from the air, and we see Climeworks and other people trying to put up these big factories, so to speak, to suck in air and take out CO2. That may or may not scale. I don't know enough about that. I always like to go to where the source of the problem is as opposed to fixing the problem to allow other people. So that's where they're at. I don't know if that's going to be great. I hope it is. We need lots of solutions. There's not one silver bullet. So I hope they're very successful and the competition around that success. But there's another one, which is taking the carbon that's already locked into plants and turning that into something that stays locked into biomass. In other words, how do we take the oil that we pumped up or coal and how do we put it back in the ground and leave it in the ground? Just like we're storing it again, not as gas or whatever. And I look at biochar. I don't know if you know about biochar, but biochar is a way to take various biomasses, you basically light them on fire, yes, but at a certain degree, certain temperature, certain airflow, and it locks all that carbon in. And once you put it on the ground, it stays there for thousands of years and then gets buried under the surface and that's what turned into oil or coal over time, which was biochar. So if we think about today, biochar has been around for thousands of years. Now we have to do it at scale. We can get energy out of it, but we can also lock that carbon in because, again, if you have dead tree trunks or whatever else, sooner or later that carbon will be emitted, maybe 50 years, 100 years, from the decaying mass. Well, today we can just take biochar, lock it in, in a very simple process, put it in the ground, get energy out, and it's a win-win for all of us. So those are the kind of things when I see carbon sequestration, that's really smart because people are like, let's just plant more trees. Yes, but once the trees fall down, we need to make sure that carbon stays out of the environment. How do we do that? Something like biochar and locking it in and storing it. There's a third one, which is carbon capture, which is just hooking on carbon filters from smokestacks or wherever it's coming out and then trying to recapture that and turning it into other products, which can be done as well. So I think those are the three different areas that I see as carbon capture and I think that all three are necessary and we should be seriously throwing those scrubbers on sooner rather than later, especially on the CO2 generators, as we start to make the transition to much cleaner hydrogen-based manufacturing processes or whatever that are not using the old CO2 emitting processes.
turns.zh: 好的。所以有从空气中移除碳的方法，我们看到 Climeworks 和其他人试图建立这些大工厂，可以说是吸入空气并取出二氧化碳。这可能会规模化，也可能不会。我对此了解不够。我总是喜欢去问题的源头，而不是为了允许其他人而修复问题。所以他们就在那里。我不知道那是否会很好。我希望是。我们需要很多解决方案。没有银弹。所以我希望他们非常成功，并且围绕这种成功展开竞争。但还有另一种方法，那就是把已经锁定在植物中的碳转化为仍然锁定在生物质中的东西。换句话说，我们如何把抽上来的石油或煤放回地下并留在那里？就像我们再次储存它，而不是作为气体或其他东西。我关注生物炭。我不知道你是否了解生物炭，但生物炭是一种方法，把各种生物质，基本上点燃它们，但在一定的程度、一定的温度、一定的气流下，它会把所有碳锁定。一旦你把它放在地上，它会停留数千年，然后被埋在地下，随着时间的推移变成石油或煤，而那就是生物炭。所以如果我们想想今天，生物炭已经存在数千年了。现在我们必须规模化地做。我们可以从中获得能量，但也可以锁定碳，因为如果你有死树干或其他东西，迟早碳会释放出来，也许 50 年、100 年，从腐烂的物质中。那么今天我们可以用生物炭，通过一个非常简单的过程锁定它，把它放在地下，获得能量，这对我们所有人都是双赢。所以当我看到碳封存时，就是这类事情，这非常聪明，因为人们说，让我们多种树。是的，但一旦树倒下，我们需要确保碳留在环境之外。我们怎么做呢？像生物炭这样的东西，锁定并储存它。还有第三种，即碳捕获，就是在烟囱或任何排放源上安装碳过滤器，然后试图重新捕获并转化为其他产品，这也可以做到。所以我认为这是我看到的碳捕获的三个不同领域，我认为所有三个都是必要的，我们应该尽早认真地安装这些洗涤器，尤其是在二氧化碳产生源上，因为我们开始向更清洁的氢基制造工艺或其他不使用旧二氧化碳排放工艺的过渡。

turns.en: Are there any other companies, startups, or larger ones? Although startups these days are getting Series A funding at billions and billions of dollars, so it's hard for me to even wrap my head around a lot of that. But are there any companies that stand out for you in those categories? I want to say that Charm Industrial would fall into the second category, I believe, and I've been quite impressed by them, with the caveat that I'm just an amateur who's still learning on the job. But any companies that you'd like to mention that you think are doing a good job?
turns.zh: 还有其他公司、初创公司或大公司吗？虽然现在的初创公司 A 轮融资就达到数十亿美元，所以我很难理解很多这些。但在这些类别中，有没有让你印象深刻的公司？我想说 Charm Industrial 属于第二类，我相信，我对他们印象深刻，但前提是我只是一个还在工作中学习的业余爱好者。但有没有你想提到的你认为做得好的公司？

turns.en: These are all small companies. Let's be very clear. They're trying to do the right thing. They want to do the right thing.
turns.zh: 这些都是小公司。让我们说清楚。他们试图做正确的事情。他们想做正确的事情。

turns.en: And now people are really starting to rally around them. Investors are the early money. And so, there's another one called Carboculture. They're also doing the biochar. So, Carboculture is there. And they're doing it en masse and they have their first factory. But again, I want to see a factory for a factory. How are they going to make lots of these so they can deploy them everywhere?
turns.zh: 现在人们真的开始围绕它们集结了。投资者是早期资金。还有一家叫 Carboculture，也在做生物炭。Carboculture 就在那里，他们正在大规模生产，并且已经有了第一家工厂。但我还想看到“工厂的工厂”。他们如何大量制造这些设备，以便到处部署？

turns.en: Yeah, right. I'm seeing a lot of new technology. I don't have any specific companies, unfortunately, right now that I can talk about in the kind of the smokestack scrubbing. There's a lot of new technology in terms of membrane technology, other kinds of chemical treatment technology to extract that CO2 from those emissions and bring them right down into some useful form. So, I don't have anything right off hand. I should go do some lookup and I'll give you some things you can put in your comments or in your description. The show notes, yeah. We'll put some links in. You know, I heard of one recently and I can't speak to the company. I haven't done any diligence on this at all, but great name. It's called Remora Carbon, r e m o r a, but it's a reference to this uh-huh. I believe it'd be categorized as a fish that kind of hangs on the sides of sharks and eats the scraps as the sharks consume their own food. And it's carbon capture for semi-trucks. So, it's scrubbing technology that is piggybacking, just like a remora in the ocean, on semi-trailer trucks. So, it's super super exciting. I mean, a lot of innovation happening right now. Like you said though, at very much so on a small scale. So how do you make a factory for a factory? Is a great way to put it.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。我看到了很多新技术。不幸的是，我现在没有具体的公司可以谈论关于烟囱洗涤方面。有很多新技术，比如膜技术、其他化学处理技术，用于从排放物中提取二氧化碳并将其转化为有用的形式。所以我手头没有现成的信息。我应该去查一下，然后给你一些可以放在评论或描述里的内容。节目笔记里，我们会放一些链接。我最近听说了一家，但我不能评价那家公司，我完全没有做过尽职调查，但名字很棒。它叫 Remora Carbon，拼写是 r e m o r a，这指的是——嗯——一种鱼，它会挂在鲨鱼侧面，吃鲨鱼进食时掉落的残渣。这是针对半挂卡车的碳捕获技术。所以这是一种搭便车的洗涤技术，就像海洋中的䲟鱼一样，附着在半挂卡车上。这非常非常令人兴奋。我的意思是，现在有很多创新。但就像你说的，规模还很小。所以“如何制造工厂的工厂”这个问题提得很好。

turns.en: Exactly. And so Remora they're doing it for trucks. You know, I think those are something that can actually move to hydrogen or ammonia kind of propulsion. They don't necessarily have to use diesel, right? And you know, cuz those are those are longer haul stuff. Same thing with electric trains, electric trains or hybrid trains. So yes, there are a lot of these things and some of them are stopgaps for the next 5 to 10 years while we transition away from petrol-based economy to a more electron-based economy.
turns.zh: 完全正确。所以 Remora 是为卡车做的。我认为这些卡车实际上可以转向氢或氨推进。它们不一定非得用柴油，对吧？因为那些是长途运输的东西。同样，电动火车、电动火车或混合动力火车也是如此。所以是的，有很多这样的东西，其中一些是未来 5 到 10 年的权宜之计，因为我们正在从石油经济过渡到更以电子为基础的经济。

turns.en: Yeah, all of these things are really interesting. We're trying to go for the stuff that's the ultimate solution, not necessarily these kinds of windows of time. Because to me, it's like a hybrid car. Sure, you can get an electric gas hybrid car, but really it's two systems that are much more complicated, more things to break. Let's try to get to the end goal as opposed to this interim goal. So I always try to look at something where it's the destination, not the waypoint along the way.
turns.zh: 是的，所有这些都很有趣。我们试图追求的是终极解决方案，而不一定是这些时间窗口。因为对我来说，这就像混合动力汽车。当然，你可以买一辆电动汽油混合动力车，但实际上它是两个系统，复杂得多，更容易出故障。让我们努力达到最终目标，而不是这个中间目标。所以我总是试图寻找那些本身就是目的地的东西，而不是沿途的停靠点。

turns.en: As we move to electrons, I don't know if you are open to taking a stab at this, but how do you think geopolitics will figure into the supply chain for an electron-based economy, right? If you just look at electric vehicles, EV, right? You look at the raw materials, and I'm not technical enough to know what those raw materials are, but if you look at the incredibly good job that certain countries are doing, right? You can certainly name, but in Africa, in South America, at establishing China, Russia. Exactly. Really good relationships on the ground. They're building infrastructure for cities and countries, and I don't want to say in exchange, but often in tandem, developing incredibly good incredibly dominant sourcing and distribution for a lot of raw materials. So what do you think? What are some plausible ways that that develops?
turns.zh: 随着我们转向电子经济，我不知道你是否愿意尝试回答这个问题：你认为地缘政治将如何影响电子经济的供应链？就拿电动汽车来说吧。你看原材料，我技术不够，不知道那些原材料是什么，但你看某些国家做得非常好，对吧？你肯定能说出名字，但在非洲、南美洲，中国、俄罗斯正在建立非常扎实的实地关系。他们正在为城市和国家建设基础设施，我不想说是交换，但通常是同步进行，为许多原材料开发出非常出色、非常占主导地位的采购和分销网络。那么你怎么看？有哪些可能的发展方式？

turns.en: Well, I think we're seeing the reality right now with the Ukraine war that's going on. And we're starting to understand that we need to have more independence in terms of our energy supplies, our raw material supplies, and we need to be working with people that have like values, like ethics, like morals, if we're going to truly be a global economy. I think we went down that road, and we're seeing people exploiting that to their own gain for too long, and now wanting to rise up in a more powerful position, whether they can or can't. I'm not going to judge that. But I think that we need — we, being each country, needs to have their own — Europe is EU, I'm calling it, you know, I know it's many countries, but they each need to have their own strategic way of doing energy sourcing. Luckily, as we go to electrons, that's going to be easier because it can be done inside the borders. But from a materials perspective, there's going to have to be many more suppliers. And the reason being is, you know, we kind of let monopolies prevail in there, and we're like, "Oh, those are dirty. Those are hard businesses." Actually, there's a lot of interesting businesses that can be built with new technology that will actually help us to find and mine things or find these elements much more easily inside our national borders and/or our true friends' national borders, so we can get these strategic things set up. What we were witnessing in many regards, whether that's in materials, minerals, manufacturing, it was outsourced to the lowest GDP country or the lowest GDP per capita country to get that lowest cost labor, and then those things will come back to us. We're not going to see that anymore. We're going to see technology-derived extraction, these kinds of things, and it's going to have to be nationally protected. And it's just going to happen more and more.
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为我们现在从正在进行的乌克兰战争中看到了现实。我们开始明白，我们需要在能源供应和原材料供应方面更加独立，并且如果我们想要一个真正的全球经济，我们需要与拥有相同价值观、道德和伦理的人合作。我认为我们曾经走过那条路，我们看到人们太长时间地利用这一点为自己谋利，现在他们想要崛起到一个更强大的位置，无论他们能不能做到。我不评判这一点。但我认为我们需要——我们，每个国家，都需要有自己的——欧洲是欧盟，我这么称呼它，我知道它有很多国家，但他们每个都需要有自己的战略能源采购方式。幸运的是，随着我们转向电子经济，这会更容易，因为可以在国境内完成。但从材料的角度来看，将需要更多的供应商。原因是，我们有点让垄断盛行，我们觉得“哦，那些是肮脏的，那些是艰难的生意。”实际上，有很多有趣的业务可以通过新技术建立起来，这些技术实际上可以帮助我们在本国境内或真正朋友的国境内更容易地找到和开采东西或找到这些元素，这样我们就可以建立这些战略物资。我们在许多方面目睹的情况，无论是材料、矿产还是制造业，都被外包给了 GDP 最低或人均 GDP 最低的国家，以获得最低的劳动力成本，然后那些东西会回到我们这里。我们不会再看到这种情况了。我们将看到技术驱动的开采之类的东西，而且它必须受到国家保护。这种情况只会越来越多。

turns.en: Now, that's one thing, which is the supply. But if we also look at the technology, there's things like non-lithium-based batteries that are coming. Sulfur-based, sodium-based. So iron-based. So we're actually able to then gear some of the technologies for certain things to the resources that we have or the abundance of resources around the planet where it's not as strategic, right? As where we hear about the rare earths. Some are rare and some aren't, but rare earths. This is the reason why we invested in that motor company, Turntide, is because they're not using any rare earths, right? So what is that look like when you have a different way of creating those motors and different partnerships that you need to be able to do so.
turns.zh: 现在，这是一方面，即供应。但如果我们再看看技术，有一些非锂电池正在出现。硫基、钠基、铁基。所以我们实际上可以将某些技术调整为我们拥有的资源或地球上丰富的资源，这些资源不那么具有战略性，对吧？就像我们听到的稀土。有些是稀有的，有些不是，但稀土。这就是我们投资那家电机公司 Turntide 的原因，因为他们不使用任何稀土，对吧？那么当你用不同的方式制造这些电机，并且需要不同的合作伙伴时，那会是什么样子？

turns.en: So I think we're going to be spending more and more time on understanding we need to invest in strategic capabilities, and we can't just farm it out to the lowest cost in the world and use the logistics chain, and then we get trapped from the geopolitical side. And the war is horrible, it's devastating, all those other things, but with high energy prices, guess what? It makes climate change a lot easier in terms of the rational argument for why we need to do it from a business perspective. Obviously, there's an emotional and rational to preserve our species on the planet, but some people don't see it that way, especially some shareholders.
turns.zh: 所以我认为我们将花越来越多的时间来理解我们需要投资于战略能力，我们不能只是把它外包给世界上成本最低的地方，利用物流链，然后我们就会陷入地缘政治的陷阱。战争是可怕的，是毁灭性的，还有其他所有问题，但有了高能源价格，你猜怎么着？从商业角度来说，它让气候变化的理性论证变得容易得多。显然，保护我们在地球上的物种既有情感因素也有理性因素，但有些人并不这么看，尤其是一些股东。

ts.sec: Geopolitical and economic context of energy transition

turns.en: But I think we're starting to see that it's not just doing the right thing, it's not just about us as a society, it's also the right thing geopolitically to start to really figure out how to be more self-sufficient and get away from these things. And luckily, we do have technologies today that are already available to allow this kind of world to exist, even though we'd rather be all one thing. Unfortunately, not everyone sees it the same.
turns.zh: 但我认为我们开始看到，这不仅仅是做正确的事，不仅仅关乎我们作为社会整体，从地缘政治角度看，真正开始想办法实现更多自给自足、摆脱这些东西也是正确的。幸运的是，我们今天确实已经拥有可用的技术，让这样的世界成为可能，尽管我们更希望全球一体。不幸的是，并非所有人都持相同看法。

turns.en: Yeah, it's certainly when people get hit in the wallet as intensely as a lot of people have been with fuel costs, and will continue to be, it sort of highlights the vulnerabilities that we have if we don't take a lot of these newer technologies seriously and newer options for sourcing.
turns.zh: 是的，当人们像很多人那样因燃料成本而钱包大出血，并且还会继续如此时，这确实突显了我们的脆弱性——如果我们不认真对待这些新技术和新的采购选择的话。

turns.en: Right, exactly. And frankly, if you look at our petrochemical world, it's been subsidized for decades. It has been propped up, right? And if you look at when we look at inflation, I really look at it the opposite. Deflation is stopping. We've been disinflated. We've been moving everything offshore. We've been going the lowest for paying a workforce, getting rid of healthcare and all this other stuff. We've been just pushing the externalities on everyone else, what we're doing to the climate. Now it's all coming back to roost, and that deflation thing that was happening is no longer, and we're just paying back what we weren't paying all those years, right? So when people go, 'It's being done to us?' No, we did that to ourselves to try to keep prices low for so long. And now all of a sudden, we really pressed reset on the whole system, whether that's workforce, materials, manufacturing, sourcing. Now it's coming back, and we're like, 'Oh, inflation.' No, we're finally paying the prices we should have paid, maybe not all spread evenly. We should have been paying with a steady increase over time.
turns.zh: 对，完全正确。坦率地说，如果你看看我们的石化世界，它已经被补贴了几十年，一直被人为支撑着，对吧？而当我们看通胀时，我其实是从相反的角度看的。通缩正在停止。我们之前一直在通缩。我们把一切都转移到海外，我们追求最低的劳动力成本，取消医保等等。我们一直把外部成本转嫁给其他人，包括我们对气候造成的影响。现在这一切都回来了，之前的通缩不复存在，我们只是在偿还那些年没付的账，对吧？所以当人们说‘这是别人强加给我们的？’不，是我们自己为了长期压低价格而造成的。现在突然间，我们真的重置了整个系统，无论是劳动力、材料、制造还是采购。现在后果来了，我们却说‘哦，通胀。’不，我们终于支付了本应付出的价格，也许没有均匀分摊。我们本应该随着时间的推移稳步上涨的。

ts.sec: Optimism vs apathy among younger generations

turns.en: So you seem like an optimistic guy to me. Would you consider yourself optimistic?
turns.zh: 所以在我看来你似乎是个乐观的人。你觉得自己乐观吗？

turns.en: Well, the alternative sucks, so...
turns.zh: 嗯，另一种选择很糟糕，所以……

turns.en: Well, we'll say more about that. Because I think I've seen various polls and surveys of especially younger people, like 20 to 35, maybe even younger, who have record levels of apathy and pessimism. That's a problem. That is a very, very significant problem. Especially when you consider a lot of the entrepreneurs who are going to be in the full-contact professional sports of business, do a lot of great work in that band, right? And whether that is in the for-profit sector or not, but just people getting big things done in the world, a lot of them fall in that band. How do you cultivate optimism, or how would you sell optimism to people who are feeling apathetic when they're just getting barraged all day long by what they consider to be fatalistic bad news?
turns.zh: 嗯，我们再多谈谈这个。因为我看到各种民意调查显示，尤其是 20 到 35 岁甚至更年轻的群体，出现了创纪录的冷漠和悲观情绪。这是个问题，一个非常非常严重的问题。特别是考虑到很多企业家——那些将要投身商业这项高强度职业体育的人——在这个年龄段做了很多伟大的工作，对吧？无论是否在营利性领域，很多成就大事的人都处在这个年龄段。你如何培养乐观情绪，或者你会如何向那些整天被他们认为宿命的坏消息轰炸、感到冷漠的人推销乐观？

turns.en: Well, first, change your media diet. First, change your media diet and start talking and listening to people who actually have some possible solutions for the problems. Now that you're tuned into the problems that some of the problems we have, go learn about them. Go see how you can be part of the solution. So either you can sit there and be apathetic and be part of the problem, or you can move and be part of the solution, right? Apathy doesn't sit in the middle between problem and solution. If you are apathetic, you're part of the problem. You need to be working on the solutions. And the more that you look at the solutions, and I'm seeing things for 5, 7, 10 years out, 15 years out, you can't help but be optimistic because you see the stuff that's coming down through these research labs or from these brilliant entrepreneurs who have great ideas. And some will fail, of course, but a lot of them will succeed, and you start going, 'Oh my god, there is a future. We can do this if we want to embrace it.' We saw it with COVID. We all got together at COVID just 2 years ago. 'Oh my god, we're putting up temporary hospitals. What can we do about testing? What are we doing about new ways of communicating the data and getting the news stories out there?' And of course, there was misinformation, all the other stuff. But look at how the world rallied together and tried to figure this stuff out. Governments did. And you might even feel, at least I did here in Europe, a sense of leadership from the politicians and the governments of trying, even if you might not agree, trying to do something about it. When you see that we do have that capability as humans to help each other and help our own as well as each other. When we know that there are climate crisis problems out there. And when you start to see the solutions, it's hard to be pessimistic or apathetic about it. Because again, if you are, then you're not helping to solve it. Right? You're not helping to be on that side. Be on the other side helping to solve it and maybe you'll see something. I'm a cautious optimist, okay? I'm not just a... I used to be an optimistic optimist. I'm a cautious optimist, right? Or a pessimistic optimist. I make sure we pick things because there's lots of things to pick from that can be real and help those become real. But I obviously ask all the questions. It's just not like, 'Oh yeah, that's going to be great.' No, we have to really dive in because there's a lot of shady people out there selling things that don't really exist. But when you start to get on the other side of the equation and start to look for solutions, then you can start to get optimistic. So change your media diet first and foremost. Start learning. Think about things you're passionate about and dive in and learn because there's so many resources out there. You wouldn't believe how many things are out there. And people are needed for those. And they're usually really well paying, too. And you're going to do something that's meaningful to you, to your family, maybe your kids or grandkids, and it's going to help your society and it's going to help the planet. So that when you sit at a dinner table in 2050 with your grandkids or whoever it is and they go, 'Oh my god, we just got past this climate crisis issue. It's always going to be lingering with us, but we got past it.' Your kids or your generations, people around the table go, 'What did you do about it? How did you solve it personally or professionally? How are you part of the solution?' You can actually have really good answers. Because this is an existential thing. This is not about metaverse or the craziness around the NFT gambling. This is about true problems that we have. And to your earlier point, the first trillionaire will be someone who solves these massive problems on this planet that we have. Because why? Because we're not going to exist without them. And that's the biggest pain ever. Think about this.

Start with picking your information sources more carefully. And I encourage you to check out the book, Built, an Unorthodox Guide to Making Things Worth Making by Tony Fadell. And once again, till next time, thank you for tuning in. Thank you, too.
turns.zh: 致所有听众，首先，感谢你们的收听。嗯，下次再见，比必要的更友善一点，培养乐观精神。这是可以培养的。从更仔细地选择你的信息来源开始。我鼓励你们去看看这本书，《创造：非正统的值得创造之物指南》，作者托尼·法德尔。再次，下次再见，感谢收听。也谢谢你。

consensus.en: Storytelling should define the product, not just sell it. Write the press release before building.
consensus.zh: 故事讲述应定义产品，而非仅用于销售。在构建产品前先写好新闻稿。

consensus.en: Energy inefficiency causes 60% loss globally; targeting it is key to climate solutions.
consensus.zh: 全球能源效率低下导致60%的损失；解决这一问题是气候方案的关键。

consensus.en: Small teams thrive on self-management, task rotation, and clear prioritization to avoid burnout.
consensus.zh: 小团队通过自我管理、任务轮换和明确优先级来避免倦怠。

consensus.en: Optimism is cultivated by changing media diet and focusing on solutions, not problems.
consensus.zh: 乐观通过改变媒体摄入和关注解决方案而非问题来培养。

consensus.en: Hydrogen and biomass economies are underinvested areas for decarbonizing industry.
consensus.zh: 氢经济和生物质经济是工业脱碳中投资不足的领域。

contrarian.en: Being called an 'asshole' can mean you care deeply about mission and quality, not ego.
contrarian.zh: 被称为‘混蛋’可能意味着你深切关心使命和质量，而非自我。

contrarian.en: Steve Jobs' vacations were intense brainstorming sessions, not true time off.
contrarian.zh: 史蒂夫·乔布斯的假期是紧张的头脑风暴，而非真正的休息。

contrarian.en: Inflation today is actually the end of deflation from outsourcing externalities.
contrarian.zh: 今天的通胀实际上是外包外部性导致通缩结束的结果。

contrarian.en: The first trillionaire will solve climate problems because survival is the ultimate pain.
contrarian.zh: 首位万亿富翁将解决气候问题，因为生存是最大的痛点。

contrarian.en: Technology can disrupt historical cycles of world order, changing predictable arcs.
contrarian.zh: 技术可以打破世界秩序的历史周期，改变可预测的轨迹。
